metadata.source_title: Cyberattacks more dangerous due to artificial intelligence
metadata.tags: Rapport, Technologie, Media & Telecom, AI, Cybersecurity, Digitalisering, Sociaal ondernemen
metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: More than half of companies view innovations in artificial intelligence (AI) as a threat to their organisation’s cyber security.
metadata.parent_text: Last year it was not even a quarter.

text: Risk perception is strongest among the largest companies, according to a survey by ABN AMRO and research agency MWM2 among 895 organisations. AI seems to increase offensive power mainly in the area of social engineering.

metadata.source_title: Cyberattacks more dangerous due to artificial intelligence
metadata.tags: Rapport, Technologie, Media & Telecom, AI, Cybersecurity, Digitalisering, Sociaal ondernemen
metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: More than half of companies view innovations in artificial intelligence (AI) as a threat to their organisation’s cyber security.
metadata.parent_text: Risk perception is strongest among the largest companies, according to a survey by ABN AMRO and research agency MWM2 among 895 organisations. AI seems to increase offensive power mainly in the area of social engineering. This manipulates people into sharing confidential information or taking another harmful action. AI makes this manipulation even sneakier and more convincing. Generative AI, for example, makes it very easy to create [phishing](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/cybersecurity/wat-is-phishing.html) emails. This form of AI can write texts on the basis of a few instructions — grammatically correct, without spelling mistakes and in a language of choice.

text:
The use of deepfake technology — which creates image or audio material that is indistinguishable from the real thing — can make victims believe they are dealing with someone else. [Listen to the podcast as well: Generative AI: The difference between real and fake](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/in-gesprek/podcast/de-innovatiegolf/generatieve-ai-het-verschil-tussen-echt-en-nep.html)
Convincing clone of the CEO. In Hong Kong, a finance employee at a multinational transferred more than [25 million dollars](https://edition.cnn.com/2024/02/04/asia/deepfake-cfo-scam-hong-kong-intl-hnk/index.html) to fraudsters after joining a video meeting with a deepfake version of the CFO and other colleagues.

metadata.source_title: Cyberattacks more dangerous due to artificial intelligence
metadata.tags: Rapport, Technologie, Media & Telecom, AI, Cybersecurity, Digitalisering, Sociaal ondernemen
metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: More than half of companies view innovations in artificial intelligence (AI) as a threat to their organisation’s cyber security.
metadata.parent_text:
The use of deepfake technology — which creates image or audio material that is indistinguishable from the real thing — can make victims believe they are dealing with someone else. [Listen to the podcast as well: Generative AI: The difference between real and fake](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/in-gesprek/podcast/de-innovatiegolf/generatieve-ai-het-verschil-tussen-echt-en-nep.html)
Convincing clone of the CEO. In Hong Kong, a finance employee at a multinational transferred more than [25 million dollars](https://edition.cnn.com/2024/02/04/asia/deepfake-cfo-scam-hong-kong-intl-hnk/index.html) to fraudsters after joining a video meeting with a deepfake version of the CFO and other colleagues. In October last year, Dutch online bank Bunq faced a similar attempt when an employee was invited to a video call with — as it later turned out — an AI clone of CEO Ali Niknam. In a [LinkedIn post](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7114874320627085312/) Niknam calls the clone “convincing”. [Read also: Sharp rise in CEO fraud](/nl/zakelijk/insights/cybersecurity/phishing/sterke-toename-in-ceo-fraude.html). According to the UK’s [National Cyber Security Centre](https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/report/impact-of-ai-on-cyber-threat#section_5) (NCSC), all types of attackers will benefit from these new social engineering possibilities, from advanced state hackers to less capable cybercriminals. Automatic scanning of company data for value will also become easier for all malicious actors, so the damage from data breaches is likely to grow in the coming years.

Automatic scanning of company data for value will also become easier for all malicious actors, so the damage from data breaches is likely to grow in the coming years.

text: AI makes capable attackers even stronger. Generative AI is also used to write malware.

Data breaches caused by chatbots. Companies that use smart chatbots based on generative AI risk employees sharing confidential information in these conversations. Samsung halted employees’ use of generative AI tools after it emerged that one of them had entered a piece of source code into ChatGPT in the hope the chatbot could find a solution for a bug in the code. Transcripts of internal meetings also ended up in the chats.

metadata.source_title: Cyberattacks more dangerous due to artificial intelligence
metadata.tags: Rapport, Technologie, Media & Telecom, AI, Cybersecurity, Digitalisering, Sociaal ondernemen
metadata.author: Julia Krauwer

metadata.parent_text: In this way, sensitive data can leak not only to the AI provider, but information may also be shared unintentionally with other users. In addition, conversations can be used again as training data to refine the AI model, and that training data does not always appear to be well locked down. At the end of last year, Google researchers revealed how they had coaxed competitor OpenAI’s ChatGPT into [disclosing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.17035) training data. The risk of data breaches via chatbots is therefore real. AI as a weapon against cybercrime. AI can also be used to improve cyber security. “It is always the crooks who are first to embrace a particular technology, but it never takes long before the defence starts working with it too,” says Arwi van der Sluijs, Managing Director of cybersecurity service provider NFIR. AI can help to identify new patterns that indicate suspicious activity in a network. Previously, people identified such patterns by manually studying large amounts of data and doing desk research. Now this can be largely automated. “Stronger still: thanks to AI we can start to predict incidents.”. The current dynamic is increasingly starting to look like an arms race.

AI can help to identify new patterns that indicate suspicious activity in a network. Previously, people identified such patterns by manually studying large amounts of data and doing desk research. Now this can be largely automated. “Stronger still: thanks to AI we can start to predict incidents.”. The current dynamic is increasingly starting to look like an arms race.
